infobox.value: ([/ˈnɒrəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NORR-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a village and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), England, 7 miles (11 km) south-west of [Berwick](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") on the south side of the [River Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") where it is the border with Scotland.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Norham** ([/ˈnɒrəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NORR-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a village and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), England, 7 miles (11 km) south-west of [Berwick](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") on the south side of the [River Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") where it is the border with Scotland.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Its ancient name was Ubbanford. [Ecgred of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Ecgred_of_Lindisfarne "Ecgred of Lindisfarne") (d.845) replaced a wooden church with one of stone and translated the relics of St Ceolwulf to it.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Norham is mentioned as the resting place of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") in the early eleventh century text *[On the Resting-Places of the Saints](/wiki/On_the_Resting-Places_of_the_Saints "On the Resting-Places of the Saints")*, and recent research has suggested the possibility that Norham (rather than [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") or [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England")) may have been the centre of the [diocese of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Diocese_of_Lindisfarne "Diocese of Lindisfarne") from the ninth century until some time between 1013 and 1031.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

It is the site of the 12th-century [Norham Castle](/wiki/Norham_Castle "Norham Castle") and was for many years the centre of the [Norhamshire](/wiki/Norhamshire "Norhamshire") [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"). It was transferred to Northumberland in 1844.

The 12th century also saw the construction of the parish church of St Cuthbert, an ambitious work with an aisled nave and long chancel, heavily rebuilt in 1846-52. As may be expected from a possession of the bishops of Durham, the details echo contemporary work in [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

It was on the Tweed here that [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") met the Scots nobility in 1292 to decide on the future king of Scotland.

[Sir Walter Scott](/wiki/Sir_Walter_Scott "Sir Walter Scott") gained fame as a poet, particularly with *[Marmion](/wiki/Marmion_(poem) "Marmion (poem)")* set around the [Battle of Flodden](/wiki/Battle_of_Flodden "Battle of Flodden") in 1513. It begins:

:   :   :   :   *Day set on Norham's castled steep,*
            :   *And Tweed's fair river, broad and deep,*
            :   *And Cheviot's mountains lone:*
            :   *The battled towers, the donjon keep,*
            :   *The loophole grates where captives weep,*
            :   *The flanking walls that round it sweep,*
            :   *In yellow lustre shone.*

The 19th-century [Ladykirk and Norham Bridge](/wiki/Ladykirk_and_Norham_Bridge "Ladykirk and Norham Bridge") is a late stone [road bridge](/wiki/Road_bridge "Road bridge") that connects the village with [Ladykirk](/wiki/Ladykirk,_Scottish_Borders "Ladykirk, Scottish Borders") in the [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner_-_Norham_Castle%2C_Sunrise_-_WGA23182.jpg/250px-Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner_-_Norham_Castle%2C_Sunrise_-_WGA23182.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Mallord_William_Turner_-_Norham_Castle,_Sunrise_-_WGA23182.jpg)

*[Norham Castle, Sunrise](/wiki/Norham_Castle,_Sunrise "Norham Castle, Sunrise")* by [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner")

[J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") reportedly tipped his hat to Norham Castle in 1831, as it was the place that had brought him fame as an artist in 1798.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was a subject he revisited throughout his career. The painting of the castle that hangs in [Tate Britain](/wiki/Tate_Britain "Tate Britain"), *[Norham Castle, Sunrise](/wiki/Norham_Castle,_Sunrise "Norham Castle, Sunrise")* (1845), luminously near-abstract, is one of the great treasures of the collection.

Norham railway station, built 1851, closed in 1965 and was turned into a museum by its final station master, Peter Short. In 2013 it was up for sale at an asking price of £420,000.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governance")]

An [electoral ward](/wiki/Wards_and_electoral_divisions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards and electoral divisions of the United Kingdom") in the name of Norham and Islandshires exists. This ward stretches south east to just short of [Bamburgh](/wiki/Bamburgh "Bamburgh") and has a total population taken at the 2011 Census of 4,438.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

Norham appears, under the name of Ubbanford, in *The Bernicia Chronicles* series of historical novels by Matthew Harffy, where it is the residence and seat of power of the series' protagonist, Beobrand.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Norham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]
